title: Packaging structure and manufacturing method thereof
abstract: The disclosed embodiments are related to a packaging structure and a manufacturing method thereof. A packaging structure includes a circuit element, a first semiconductor die, a second semiconductor die, a heat dissipation element, and an insulating encapsulation body. The first semiconductor die and the second semiconductor die are located on the circuit element. The heat dissipation element is connected to the first semiconductor die, and the first semiconductor die is located between the circuit element and the heat dissipation element, wherein the sum of the first thickness of the first semiconductor die and the third thickness of the heat dissipation element is substantially equal to the second thickness of the second semiconductor die. The insulating encapsulation body encapsulates the first semiconductor die, the second semiconductor die, and the heat dissipation element, wherein the surface of the heat dissipation element is substantially flush with the insulating encapsulation body.
claims: 2.根据权利要求1所述的封装结构，其中在剖面图中，所述散热元件的侧壁与所述第一半导体管芯的侧壁实质上对齐。2 . The package structure of claim 1 , wherein in a cross-sectional view, a sidewall of the heat dissipation element is substantially aligned with a sidewall of the first semiconductor die.

description:
Package structure and method for manufacturing the same
TechnicalâField
The embodiment of the disclosure relates to a packaging structure and a manufacturing method thereof.
Background
Semiconductor devices and integrated circuits (INTEGRATED CIRCUIT, ICs) are typically fabricated on a single semiconductor wafer. In wafer level processes, the die of a wafer may be processed and packaged with other semiconductor devices or other dies, and various techniques for wafer level packaging (e.g., forming rerouting circuit structures/layers) have been developed. In addition, such packages may be further integrated/integrated into a semiconductor substrate or carrier after dicing (dicing).
Disclosure of Invention
Embodiments of the present disclosure provide a package structure including a circuit element, a first semiconductor die, a second semiconductor die, a heat dissipating element, and an insulating encapsulant. The first semiconductor die and the second semiconductor die are located on the circuit element. The heat spreading element is connected to the first semiconductor die and the first semiconductor die is located between the circuit element and the heat spreading element, wherein a sum of a first thickness of the first semiconductor die and a third thickness of the heat spreading element is substantially equal to a second thickness of the second semiconductor die. The insulating encapsulant encapsulates the first semiconductor die, the second semiconductor die, and the heat dissipation element, wherein a surface of the heat dissipation element is substantially flush with a surface of the insulating encapsulant.
The embodiment of the disclosure provides a method for manufacturing a packaging structure, which comprises the steps of providing a first semiconductor die connected with a heat dissipation element, providing a second semiconductor die, packaging the first semiconductor die, the second semiconductor die and the heat dissipation element in an insulating packaging body, and flattening the insulating packaging body to enable the insulating packaging body to expose the heat dissipation element, wherein the sum of the first thickness of the first semiconductor die and the third thickness of the heat dissipation element is substantially equal to the second thickness of the second semiconductor die.
The embodiment of the disclosure provides a method for manufacturing a packaging structure, which comprises the steps of providing at least one first semiconductor die and at least one second semiconductor die, arranging a dummy element on the surface of the at least one first semiconductor die, wherein the at least one first semiconductor die is positioned above the dummy element, packaging the at least one first semiconductor die, the at least one second semiconductor die and the dummy element in an insulating packaging body, flattening the insulating packaging body to expose the dummy element on the surface of the insulating packaging body, removing the dummy element to form a groove in the insulating packaging body, and arranging a heat dissipation element on the at least one first semiconductor die and in the groove, wherein the sum of the first thickness of the at least one first semiconductor die and the third thickness of the heat dissipation element is substantially equal to the second thickness of the at least one second semiconductor die.

DetailedâDescription
The following disclosure provides many different embodiments, or examples, for implementing different features of the provided subject matter. Specific examples of components, values, operations, materials, arrangements, etc. are set forth below to simplify the present disclosure. Of course, these are merely examples and are not intended to be limiting. Other components, values, operations, materials, arrangements, etc. are contemplated. For example, forming a first feature over or on a second feature in the description that follows may include embodiments in which the first and second features are formed in direct contact, and may also include embodiments in which additional features may be formed between the first and second features, such that the first and second features may not be in direct contact. In addition, the present disclosure may repeat reference numerals and/or letters in the various examples. Such reuse is for brevity and clarity purposes and does not itself dictate a relationship between the various embodiments and/or configurations discussed.
Further, for ease of description, spatially relative terms such as "below", "above", "upper", and the like may be used herein to describe one element or feature's relationship to another element or feature as illustrated in the figures. The spatially relative terms are intended to encompass different orientations of the device in use or operation in addition to the orientation depicted in the figures. The device may have other orientations (rotated 90 degrees or at other orientations), and the spatially relative descriptors used herein interpreted accordingly.
In addition, for ease of description, terms such as "first," "second," "third," "fourth," "fifth (fifth)" and the like may be used herein to describe similar elements or features or different elements or features illustrated in the figures, and may be used interchangeably depending on the order of presentation or the context of the description.
Other features and processes may also be included in the present disclosure. For example, test structures may be included to facilitate verification testing of three-dimensional (3D) packages or three-dimensional integrated circuit (3 DIC) devices. The test structures may include test pads formed, for example, in a redistribution layer or on a substrate to enable testing of 3D packages or 3DIC, use of probes and/or probe cards (probe cards), and the like. Verification tests may be performed on intermediate structures as well as final structures. Additionally, the structures and methods disclosed herein may be used in conjunction with test methods that include intermediate verification of known good die (knowngood die) to improve yield (yield) and reduce cost.

Referring to fig. 1, in some embodiments, a wafer W1 comprising a plurality of semiconductor die 100 arranged in an array is provided according to step S10 of fig. 16. Before performing a wafer sawing (dicing) or dicing process on the wafer W1, the semiconductor die 100 of the wafer W1 are connected to each other, as shown in fig. 1. In some embodiments, the wafer W1 includes a semiconductor substrate 110, an interconnect structure 120 disposed on the semiconductor substrate 110, and a passivation layer 130 covering the interconnect structure 120. As shown in fig. 1, for example, the semiconductor substrate 110 has a top surface 110a and a bottom surface 110b opposite the top surface 110a, and the interconnect structure 120 is located on the top surface 110a of the semiconductor substrate 110 and is sandwiched between the semiconductor substrate 110 and the passivation layer 130.
In some embodiments, the semiconductor substrate 110 may be a silicon substrate including active components (e.g., transistors and/or memory (e.g., N-type metal oxide semiconductor (N-TYPE METAL-oxide semiconductor, NMOS) and/or P-type metal oxide semiconductor (P-TYPE METAL-oxide semiconductor, PMOS) devices, etc.) and/or passive components (e.g., resistors, capacitors, inductors, etc.) formed therein. In some embodiments, such active and passive components may be formed in front-end-of-line (FEOL) processes. In alternative embodiments, the semiconductor substrate 110 may be a bulk silicon substrate, such as a bulk monocrystalline silicon substrate, a doped silicon substrate, an undoped silicon substrate, or a silicon-on-insulator (SOI) substrate, wherein the dopant of the doped silicon substrate may be an N-type dopant, a P-type dopant, or a combination thereof. The present disclosure is not limited thereto.
The semiconductor substrate 110 may comprise, for example, other semiconductor materials including germanium, compound semiconductors including silicon carbide, gallium arsenide, gallium phosphide, indium arsenide, and/or indium antimonide, alloy semiconductors including SiGe, gaAsP, alInAs, alGaAs, gaInAs, gaInP, and/or GaInAsP, or combinations thereof. Other substrates may also be used, such as a multi-layer (multi-layered) substrate or a gradient substrate (gradient substrate). The semiconductor substrate 110 has an active surface (e.g., top surface 110 a), sometimes referred to as a front side, and an inactive surface (e.g., bottom surface 110 b), sometimes referred to as a back side.
In some embodiments, the interconnect structure 120 may include one or more interlayer dielectric layers 122 and one or more patterned conductive layers 124 alternately stacked. For example, the interlayer dielectric layer 122 may be a silicon oxide layer, a silicon nitride layer, a silicon oxynitride layer, or a dielectric layer formed of other suitable dielectric material, and the interlayer dielectric layer 122 may be formed by deposition or the like. For example, the patterned conductive layer 124 may be a patterned copper layer or other suitable patterned metal layer, and the patterned conductive layer 124 may be formed by electroplating or deposition. However, the present disclosure is not limited thereto. In some embodiments, the patterned conductive layer 124 may be formed by a dual damascene method. The number of interlayer dielectric layers 122 and the number of patterned conductive layers 124 may be less or greater than those depicted in fig. 1 and may be specified based on needs and/or design layout, although the disclosure is not particularly limited thereto. Throughout the description, the term "copper" is intended to include substantially pure elemental copper, copper containing unavoidable impurities, and copper alloys containing small amounts of elements (e.g., tantalum, indium, tin, zinc, manganese, chromium, titanium, germanium, strontium, platinum, magnesium, aluminum, or zirconium, etc.).
In some embodiments, as shown in fig. 1, the patterned conductive layer 124 is interposed between the interlayer dielectric layers 122, wherein a top surface of a topmost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 is at least partially exposed by a plurality of openings O1 formed in the topmost layer of the interlayer dielectric layer 122 to connect to later formed components for electrical connection, and a bottom surface of a lowermost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 is at least partially exposed by a plurality of openings (not labeled) formed in the lowermost layer of the interlayer dielectric layer 122 and electrically connected to active components and/or passive components included in the semiconductor substrate 110. The shape and number of openings O1 and openings formed in the lowermost layer of the interlayer dielectric layer 122 are not limited in this disclosure and may be specified based on needs and/or design layout.
The present disclosure is not limited thereto. In an alternative embodiment (not shown), the patterned conductive layer 124 may be interposed between the interlayer dielectric layers 122, wherein a top surface of a topmost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 may be completely covered by the interlayer dielectric layer 122 to prevent damage caused by subsequent processes, and a bottom surface of a lowermost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 may be at least partially exposed by an opening formed in the lowermost layer of the interlayer dielectric layer 122 and electrically connected to an underlying active component and/or passive component. For example, by a subsequent patterning process, a top surface of the topmost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 covered by the topmost layer of the interlayer dielectric layer 122 may be exposed by a plurality of openings formed in the topmost layer of the interlayer dielectric layer 122 to electrically connect to a component formed later overlying the top surface of the topmost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124.
In some embodiments, as shown in fig. 1, a passivation layer 130 is formed on the interconnect structure 120, wherein the interconnect structure 120 is covered by the passivation layer 130 and is in contact with the passivation layer 130. As shown in fig. 1, for example, the passivation layer 130 has a substantially planar surface 130a. In certain embodiments, the surface 130a of the passivation layer 130 may be flush and may have high flatness and high planarity, which is beneficial for later formed layers. In some embodiments, the passivation layer 130 may be a Polyimide (PI) layer, a Polybenzoxazole (PBO) layer, a silicon dioxide-based (non-organic) layer, or other suitable polymer (or organic) layer, and may be formed by deposition or the like. The present disclosure is not limited thereto. In some embodiments, the passivation layer 130 completely covers the topmost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 and the topmost layer of the interlayer dielectric layer 122, wherein the interconnect structure 120 is well protected from damage caused by subsequent processes by the passivation layer 130. As shown in fig. 1, for example, the interconnect structure 120 is located between the top surface 110a of the semiconductor substrate 110 and the passivation layer 130. The thickness of the passivation layer 130 is not particularly limited in the present disclosure as long as the surface 130a of the passivation layer 130 can maintain its high flatness and high planarity. In this disclosure, the surface 130a of the passivation layer may be referred to as the top surface of the semiconductor die 100.
Continuing with fig. 1, in some embodiments, according to step S11 of fig. 16, a wafer W2 comprising a plurality of heat dissipating elements HDE arranged in an array is also provided. Before performing a wafer sawing or dicing process on the wafer W2, the heat dissipation elements HDE of the wafer W2 are connected to each other as shown in fig. 1. In the present disclosure, the heat sink element HDE is also referred to as a heat sink element (HEAT SPREADER ELEMENT). In some embodiments, the wafer W2 includes a base layer 210 and an adhesive layer 220 coated on the base layer 210. As shown in fig. 1, for example, substrate layer 210 has a top surface 210a and a bottom surface 210b opposite top surface 210a, and an adhesive layer 220 is formed on top surface 210a of substrate layer 210. In the present disclosure, the material of the base layer 210 is different from the material of the adhesive layer 220. In the embodiment where the wafer W2 includes the base layer 210 and the adhesive layer 220, the base layer 210 and the adhesive layer 220 included in the wafer W2 are referred to as a heat sink HDE together in the present disclosure.
However, the present disclosure is not limited thereto, and in alternative embodiments, the adhesive layer 220 may be omitted from the wafer W2. That is, in embodiments where wafer W2 does not include adhesive layer 220, base layer 210 included in wafer W2 is referred to alone in this disclosure as a heat sink HDE.
In some embodiments, given that base layer 210 has a greater thermal conductivity (thermal conductivity coefficient) than the later formed insulating encapsulation, base layer 210 may include a silicon-based layer (e.g., a silicon substrate or silicon oxide layer), a ceramic layer, a metal film (e.g., a copper (Cu) film, an aluminum (Al) film, an indium (In) film, or a silver (Ag) film), a layer formed from a metal paste (e.g., an Ag paste, a Cu paste, a nano-Ag paste, or a nano-Cu paste), a polymer layer, a compound layer, or a layer made from any suitable other material.
In some embodiments, given that adhesion layer 220 has a greater thermal conductivity than the later-formed insulating encapsulant and is capable of adhering base layer 210 to any layer or any wafer (e.g., wafer W1) to be disposed over base layer 210, adhesion layer 220 may include a silicon-based layer (e.g., a silicon oxide layer, a silicon nitride layer), a layer formed of a metal paste, a polymer layer, or a layer made of any suitable other material. For example, the adhesive layer 220 may be a die attach film (DIE ATTACH FILM, DAF), a liquid DAF, a glue layer, or an adhesive tape (ADHESIVE TAPE). In some embodiments, adhesive layer 220 may be formed on substrate layer 210 by lamination (lamination), spin coating, dip coating, or a suitable coating method. For example, adhesive layer 220 may be dispensed as a liquid (e.g., liquid DAF) on substrate layer 210 and cured, or may be a laminate film (e.g., DAF) laminated to substrate layer 210. The present disclosure is not limited thereto. The top surface 220a of the adhesive layer 220 may be flush and may have a high coplanarity.
Referring to fig. 1 and 2 together, in some embodiments, a wafer W1 is picked up and placed on a wafer W2 and the wafer W1 is bonded to the wafer W2 according to step S11 of fig. 16. For example, the total thickness T1 of the structure depicted in fig. 2 ranges from 100 micrometers (Î¼m) to 2000 Î¼m. As shown in fig. 2, the semiconductor substrate 110 is located between the interconnect structure 120 and the base layer 210, the interconnect structure 120 is located between the passivation layer 130 and the semiconductor substrate 110, and the adhesion layer 220 is located between the semiconductor substrate 110 and the base layer 210. In the present disclosure, the base layer 210 is thermally coupled to the semiconductor substrate 110 of the wafer W1 independently of the adhesion layer 220 (if any). In other words, the heat sink element HDE included in the wafer W2 is thermally coupled to each of the semiconductor dies 100 included in the wafer W1.
In some embodiments, as shown in fig. 1 and 2, the bottom surface 110b of the semiconductor substrate 110 of the wafer W1 faces the top surface 220a of the adhesion layer 220 of the wafer W2, wherein the wafer W1 is bonded to the wafer W2 by bonding the semiconductor substrate 110 to the base layer 210 by means of the adhesion layer 220 (e.g., an indirect bonding method). In one embodiment, when the semiconductor substrate 110 of the wafer W1 is bonded to the base layer 210 of the wafer W2 using the adhesive layer 220 made of a material having adhesive properties (e.g., metal paste, polymer, etc.), the wafer W1 is bonded to the wafer W2 by adhesion (adhesion). In an alternative embodiment, when the semiconductor substrate 110 of the wafer W1 is bonded to the base layer 210 of the wafer W2 using the adhesive layer 220 made of a material (e.g., a silicon-based layer), the wafer W1 is bonded to the wafer W2 by fusion bonding. That is, the semiconductor substrate 110 of the wafer W1 is indirectly bonded to the base layer 210 of the wafer W2 by adhesive or fusion bonding through the adhesive layer 220. It should be appreciated that in embodiments of the present disclosure, the material of base layer 210 is different from the material of adhesive layer 220 due to the presence of adhesive layer 220.
However, the present disclosure is not limited thereto, and in alternative embodiments in which the adhesive layer 220 is omitted, the wafer W1 may be directly bonded to the wafer W2 by adhesive or fusion bonding. That is, the bottom surface 110b of the semiconductor substrate 110 of the wafer W1 faces the top surface 210a of the base layer 210 of the wafer W2, wherein the wafer W1 is bonded to the wafer W2 by physically bonding the bottom surface 110b of the semiconductor substrate 110 to the top surface 210a of the base layer 210 (e.g., a direct bonding method). In one embodiment, wafer W1 is bonded to wafer W2 by adhesion when base layer 210 made of a material having adhesive properties (e.g., metal paste, polymer, etc.) is used to directly bond to semiconductor substrate 110 of wafer W1. In an alternative embodiment, wafer W1 is bonded to wafer W2 by fusion bonding when base layer 210, which is made of a material (e.g., a silicon-based layer), is used to directly bond to semiconductor substrate 110 of wafer W1. That is, the semiconductor substrate 110 of the wafer W1 is directly bonded to the base layer 210 of the wafer W2 by adhesive or fusion bonding due to the base layer 210.
Referring to fig. 3, in some embodiments, a planarization step is performed on bottom surface 210b of base layer 210. After planarization, the base layer 210 may be considered a planarized base layer or a thinned base layer. In some embodiments, the total thickness T1' of the planarized structure depicted in fig. 3 may range from 100 Î¼m to 1200 Î¼m. In some embodiments, the planarizing step may include an abrasive process or a Chemical Mechanical Polishing (CMP) process. After the planarization step, a cleaning step, such as cleaning and removing residues generated by the planarization step, may be performed as needed. However, the present disclosure is not limited thereto, and the planarizing step may be performed by any other suitable method.
In some embodiments, to facilitate the process depicted in fig. 3 described above, the structure depicted in fig. 2 may be temporarily bonded to a support (not shown) or to a temporary carrier (not shown, e.g., an adhesive tape, adhesive carrier, suction pad, etc.) by contacting the passivation layer 130 with an adhesive film (not shown). However, the present disclosure is not limited thereto. In one embodiment, when the thickness of the semiconductor substrate 110 is sufficiently thick to perform the process depicted in fig. 3 described above without damage (e.g., cracking or broken wafers), then there is no need to temporarily bond the passivation layer 130 to the support.
Referring to fig. 4, in some embodiments, a plurality of via holes 140 are formed on the interconnect structure 120 and on the semiconductor substrate 110, and sidewalls of the via holes 140 are surrounded by the passivation layer 130. In some embodiments, as shown in fig. 4, the vias 140 each penetrate the passivation layer 130 and extend into the opening O1 formed in the topmost layer of the interlayer dielectric layer 122 to physically contact the top surface of the topmost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 exposed by the opening O1. Via 140 is electrically connected to active and/or passive components included in semiconductor substrate 110 through interconnect structure 120.
It should be appreciated that semiconductor die 100 each includes at least a semiconductor substrate 110, an interconnect structure 120, a passivation layer 130, and a via 140. In some embodiments, each of the semiconductor die 100 set forth herein may be referred to as a semiconductor chip or Integrated Circuit (IC). In some embodiments, semiconductor die 100 are each logic chips, such as a central processing unit (central processing unit, CPU), a graphics processing unit (graphic processing unit, GPU), a system-on-a-chip (SoC), a microcontroller, or the like. However, the present disclosure is not limited thereto, and in alternative embodiments, semiconductor die 100 may independently comprise one or more digital, analog, or mixed signal chips, such as application-specific integrated circuit ("ASIC") chips, sensor chips, wireless and Radio Frequency (RF) chips, memory chips, or voltage regulator chips. In some alternative embodiments, semiconductor die 100 may be independently referred to as a combined type chip or IC, such as a WiFi chip that includes both an RF chip and a digital chip.
In some embodiments, as shown in fig. 4, for each semiconductor die 100, the via 140 that is in physical contact with the interconnect structure 120 extends away from the surface 130a of the passivation layer 130. For simplicity, only two vias 140 are shown in each semiconductor die 100 in fig. 4 for illustration purposes, however, it should be noted that more than two vias 140 may be formed, and the disclosure is not limited thereto. In some embodiments, the via 140 may be independently circular, elliptical, triangular, rectangular, etc. in a perpendicular projection along the stacking direction Z of the semiconductor substrate 110, the interconnect structure 120, and the passivation layer 130 on the top surface 110a of the semiconductor substrate 110. The shape of the via 140 is not limited in this disclosure. The number and shape of the vias 140 may be specified and selected based on needs and adjusted by changing the number and shape of the openings O1.
In some embodiments, the via 140 is formed by photolithography, plating, photoresist stripping process (photoresist stripping process), or any other suitable method. For example, the plating process may include electroplating, electroless plating, and the like. In one embodiment, the via 140 may be formed by forming a mask pattern (not shown) covering the passivation layer 130, the mask pattern having a plurality of openings (not shown) corresponding to a top surface of the topmost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 exposed by the opening O1, patterning the passivation layer 130 to form a plurality of contact openings (not shown) in the passivation layer 130 to expose the top surface of the topmost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 exposed by the opening O1, forming a metal material filling the openings formed in the mask pattern, the contact openings formed in the passivation layer 130, and the opening O1 by electroplating or deposition to form the plurality of via 140, and then removing the mask pattern. In some embodiments, the passivation layer 130 may be patterned by an etching process (e.g., a dry etching process, a wet etching process, or a combination thereof). It should be noted that the contact opening formed in the passivation layer 130 and a corresponding one of the openings O1 located under the passivation layer 130 spatially communicate with each other to form the via hole 140. In one embodiment, the mask pattern may be removed by an acceptable ashing process and/or photoresist stripping process (e.g., using an oxygen plasma, etc.). In one embodiment, the material of the via 140 may include a metallic material, such as copper or copper alloy, or the like.
In some alternative embodiments, the via 140 may be formed by forming a first mask pattern (not shown) covering the passivation layer 130, the first mask pattern having a plurality of openings (not shown) corresponding to a top surface of the topmost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 exposed by the opening O1, patterning the passivation layer 130 to form a plurality of contact openings (not shown) in the passivation layer 130 to expose the top surface of the topmost layer of the patterned conductive layer 124 exposed by the opening O1, removing the first mask pattern, conformally forming a metal seed layer over the passivation layer 130, forming a second mask pattern (not shown) covering the passivation layer 130 and the metal seed layer, the second mask pattern having a plurality of openings (not shown) exposing the contact openings formed in the passivation layer 130 and the opening O1, forming a metal material filling the openings formed in the second mask pattern, the contact openings formed in the passivation layer 130 and the opening O1 by electroplating or deposition, removing the second mask pattern, and then removing the metal seed layer 140 to form the via. In some embodiments, the metal seed layer is referred to as a metal layer, which may be a single layer or a combined layer comprising multiple sub-layers formed of different materials. In some embodiments, the metal seed layer comprises titanium, copper, molybdenum, tungsten, titanium nitride, titanium tungsten, combinations thereof, and the like. For example, the metal seed layer may include a titanium layer and a copper layer over the titanium layer. The metal seed layer may be formed using, for example, sputtering, physical vapor deposition (physical vapor deposition, PVD), or the like.
Referring to fig. 5, in some embodiments, a dicing (singulation) process is performed on the structure depicted in fig. 4 according to step S12 of fig. 16, such that the structure depicted in fig. 4 is diced into individual and separate semiconductor assemblies 10a. In some embodiments, as shown in fig. 5, the holding device HD1 is employed to secure the structure depicted in fig. 4 to prevent any damage to the semiconductor component 10a during the dicing (singulation) process. In one embodiment, the holding device HD1 is not diced during the dicing (singulation) process, although the present disclosure is not limited thereto. In an alternative embodiment (not shown), the holding device HD1 may be partially cut during the dicing (singulation) process. For example, the holding device HD1 may be an adhesive tape, an adhesive carrier, or a suction pad, to which the present disclosure is not limited. In one embodiment, the dicing (singulation) process is a wafer dicing process or a wafer singulation process, which may include mechanical sawing or laser dicing. The present disclosure is not limited thereto. To this end, the semiconductor assemblies 10a are manufactured, wherein each of the semiconductor assemblies 10a includes a semiconductor die 100 to which a heat sink element HDE is connected. In the present disclosure, in each of the semiconductor components 10a, a heat sink element HDE is thermally coupled to the semiconductor die 100. As shown in fig. 5, the sidewalls of the heat sink element HDE are aligned with the sidewalls of the semiconductor die 100.
Referring to fig. 6, in some embodiments, according to step S13 of fig. 16, a substrate 310 is provided in which a semiconductor component 10a (the semiconductor component 10a depicted in fig. 5) and at least one semiconductor component 20 are picked up and placed on the substrate 310 and bonded to the substrate 310 by flip-chip bonding. In some embodiments, the substrate 310 may be a wafer, such as a bulk semiconductor substrate, an SOI substrate, a multi-layer semiconductor substrate, or the like. In some embodiments, the semiconductor material of substrate 310 may be silicon, germanium, a compound semiconductor including silicon germanium, silicon carbide, gallium arsenide, gallium phosphide, indium arsenide, and/or indium antimonide, an alloy semiconductor including SiGe, gaAsP, alInAs, alGaAs, gaInAs, gaInP, and/or GaInAsP, or a combination thereof. In alternative embodiments, other substrates, such as multi-layer substrates or gradient substrates, may also be used. The substrate 310 may be doped or undoped, for example, and may further include active devices and/or passive devices, such as transistors, capacitors, resistors, diodes, etc., formed in the substrate 310 and/or on the surface 310a of the substrate 310. In some embodiments, surface 310a may be referred to as the active surface (or front side) of substrate 310. In certain embodiments, substrate 310 may not include substantially active devices and/or passive devices, as the present disclosure is not limited in this regard.
In some embodiments, a plurality of perforations 320 extending from a surface 310a of the substrate 310 are formed in the substrate 310. For example, since the substrate 310 is a silicon substrate, the via 320 is sometimes referred to as a through-substrate-via (through-silicon-via) or a through-silicon-via (through-silicon-via). The perforations 320 may be formed by forming grooves in the substrate 310, for example, by etching, milling, laser techniques, combinations thereof, and/or the like. A thin dielectric material may be formed in the recess, for example, by using an oxidation technique. A thin barrier layer may be conformally deposited over surface 310a of substrate 310 and openings, for example, by chemical vapor deposition (chemical vapor deposition, CVD), atomic layer deposition (atomic layer deposition, ALD), PVD, thermal oxidation, combinations thereof, and/or the like. The barrier layer may comprise a nitride or oxynitride such as titanium nitride, titanium oxynitride, tantalum nitride, tantalum oxynitride, tungsten nitride, combinations thereof, and/or the like. A conductive material may be deposited over the thin barrier layer and in the opening. The conductive material may be formed by an electrochemical plating process, CVD, ALD, PVD, combinations thereof, and/or the like. Examples of conductive materials are copper, tungsten, aluminum, silver, gold, combinations thereof, and/or the like. Excess conductive material and barrier layer are removed from the surface 310a of the substrate 310 by, for example, CMP. Thus, the perforation 320 may include a conductive material and a thin barrier layer between the conductive material and the substrate 310.
In some embodiments, the rerouting circuit structure 340 is formed on the surface 310a of the substrate 310 and the rerouting circuit structure 340 is electrically connected to the substrate 310. In some embodiments, the rerouting circuit structure 340 is used to provide a routing function and includes a dielectric structure 342 and one or more metallization layers 344 disposed in the dielectric structure 342. In some embodiments, the dielectric structure 342 includes one or more dielectric layers such that the dielectric layers and the metallization layer 344 are sequentially formed with one metallization layer 344 sandwiched between two dielectric layers. As shown in fig. 6, portions of the top surface of the topmost layer of metallization layer 344 are exposed by the plurality of openings formed in the topmost dielectric layer of dielectric structure 342, respectively, and portions of the bottom surface of the bottommost layer of metallization layer 344 are exposed by the plurality of openings formed in the bottommost dielectric layer of dielectric structure 342, respectively. However, the present disclosure is not limited thereto. The number of metallization layers and the number of dielectric layers included in the rerouting circuit structure 340 are not limited thereto and may be specified and selected based on needs. The material and formation of dielectric structure 342 may be the same or similar to the material and formation of interlayer dielectric layer 122, and the material and formation of metallization layer 344 may be the same or similar to the material and formation of patterned conductive layer 124, and thus are not repeated herein. As shown in fig. 6, the vias 320 are connected to portions of the bottom surface of the bottommost layer of the metallization layer 344 that are exposed by openings formed in the bottommost dielectric layer of the dielectric structure 342, respectively. In other words, the rerouting circuit structure 340 is electrically connected to the perforation 320. In alternative embodiments, the rerouting circuit structure 340 may also be electrically connected to active devices and/or passive devices embedded in the substrate 310 or formed on the surface 310a of the substrate 310.
In some embodiments, a plurality of conductive connections 360 are formed on the rerouting circuit structure 340. As shown in fig. 6, for example, conductive connection 360 is formed on portions of the top surface of the topmost layer of metallization layer 344 that are exposed by openings formed in the topmost dielectric layer of dielectric structure 342, respectively, and connected to portions of the top surface of the topmost layer of metallization layer 344 that are exposed by openings formed in the topmost dielectric layer of dielectric structure 342, respectively. In other words, the conductive connection 360 is electrically connected to the rerouting circuit structure 340. In alternative embodiments, some of the conductive connections 360 may also be electrically connected to the substrate 310 (e.g., active devices and/or passive devices embedded in the substrate 310 or formed on the surface 310 a) and the vias 320 by re-routing the line structures 340.
In some embodiments, the conductive connection 360 may include Ball Grid Array (BGA) connections, solder balls, metal pillars, controlled collapse chip connection (controlled collapse chip connection, C4) bumps, micro-bumps, bumps formed by electroless nickel-palladium immersion gold (ENEPIG) techniques, and the like. The material of the conductive connection 360 may include, for example, a conductive material such as solder, copper, aluminum, gold, nickel, silver, palladium, tin, etc., or a combination thereof. In one embodiment, the material of the conductive connection 360 may be, for example, non-solder containing. The cross-sectional view of the conductive connection 360 is not limited to the embodiment and may have any suitable shape as desired.
Continuing with fig. 6, in some embodiments, each of the semiconductor components 20 set forth herein may be referred to as a semiconductor chip or Integrated Circuit (IC) having a plurality of vias 22, wherein the vias 22 serve as conductive terminals of the semiconductor components 20 to electrically connect to external components. In some embodiments, the underfill UF1 fills at least the gap between the semiconductor device 10a and the redistribution structure 340 and the gap between the semiconductor device 20 and the redistribution structure 340, and surrounds the sidewalls of the via 140, the sidewall of the via 22, and the sidewall of the conductive connection 360. Referring to fig. 7, in some embodiments, an insulating encapsulation 400m is formed over the substrate 310 according to step S14 of fig. 16. For example, as shown in fig. 7, the semiconductor device 10a, the semiconductor device 20 are encapsulated in an insulating encapsulation 400m, and the rerouting circuit structure 340 exposed by the underfill UF1 is covered by the insulating encapsulation 400m. For example, the insulating encapsulation 400m fills at least the gaps between the semiconductor elements 10a, 20 and the gaps between the underfill UF1 under the semiconductor elements 10a, 20, respectively. In some embodiments, the insulating encapsulation 400m is a molding compound formed by a molding process. In some embodiments, the insulating encapsulation 400m may comprise, for example, a polymer (e.g., epoxy, phenolic, silicone, or other suitable resin), a dielectric material, or other suitable material. In alternative embodiments, the insulating encapsulation 400m may comprise acceptable insulating encapsulation materials. In some embodiments, the insulating encapsulation 400m may further comprise an inorganic filler or an inorganic compound (e.g., silica, clay, etc.) that may be added in the insulating encapsulation 400m to optimize the coefficient of thermal expansion (coefficient of thermal expansion, CTE) of the insulating encapsulation 400 m. The present disclosure is not limited thereto.
Referring to fig. 7 and 8 together, in some embodiments, according to step S15 of fig. 16, the insulating encapsulation 400m is planarized until the bottom surface of the semiconductor component 10a (e.g., the bottom surface 210b of the base layer 210) and the bottom surface 20b of the semiconductor component 20 are exposed. After planarizing the insulating encapsulation 400m, an (planarized) insulating encapsulation 400 is formed, and the bottom surface of the semiconductor component 10a (e.g., the bottom surface 210b of the base layer 210) and the bottom surface 20b of the semiconductor component 20 are exposed by the surface 400a of the insulating encapsulation 400.
The semiconductor elements 10a, 20 may also be planarized independently during the planarization process of the insulating encapsulation 400 m. For example, the insulating encapsulation 400 may be formed by mechanical polishing or CMP. After the planarization process, a cleaning step, such as cleaning and removing residues generated by the planarization step, may be performed as needed. Referring to fig. 36, in some embodiments, the package structure P4A includes a semiconductor component 10a, a semiconductor component 30, an insulating encapsulation 700, a rerouting circuit structure 800, and a plurality of conductive terminals 900. In some embodiments, the rerouting circuit structure 800 is located on a surface 700a of the insulating encapsulation 700, wherein the surface 700a is opposite to the surface 700b along a stacking direction Z of the rerouting circuit structure 800 and the insulating encapsulation 700. As shown in fig. 36, for example, the rerouting circuit structure 800 is electrically connected to the semiconductor element 10a and the semiconductor element 30. In the present disclosure, the rerouting circuit structure 800 provides a routing function to the semiconductor components 10a, 30. In some embodiments, the conductive terminal 900 is located on the rerouting circuit structure 800 and connected to the rerouting circuit structure 800, where the rerouting circuit structure 800 is located between the insulating enclosure 700 and the conductive terminal 900. As shown in fig. 36, the semiconductor assembly 10a includes a first semiconductor die 100 and a heat sink element HDE disposed on the first semiconductor die 100, wherein the heat sink element HDE is thermally coupled to the first semiconductor die 100. Due to the heat sink HDE, heat generated from the semiconductor die 100 can be easily dissipated to the external environment through the heat sink HDE, thereby helping to maintain a lower temperature within the package structure P4A. That is, based on the heat dissipation element HDE of the semiconductor device 10a included in the package structure P4A, the heat dissipation efficiency of the semiconductor device 10a can be controlled by adjusting the material of the heat dissipation element HDE, thereby ensuring the reliability of the package structure P4A.
